title: As AI agents become employees, NewCore emerges with $66M to give them identities
slides: What if your AI coworker has no legal identity?
NewCore just raised $66M to fix that gap.
They issue verifiable credentials for autonomous agents.
Now contracts signed by AIs can hold legal weight.
Your next hire might never have a human name.
thread_text:
1. What if your AI coworker has no legal identity?

NewCore just raised $66M to fix that gap — they issue verifiable credentials for autonomous agents.

2. Contracts signed by AIs can now hold legal weight.

The credentials anchor agent actions to verifiable identities, making digital signatures enforceable under law.

3. Your next hire might never have a human name.

Companies are already testing agents that negotiate deals and execute contracts without a legal person behind them.

4. The $66M bet says this is coming fast.

NewCore's funding round shows investors believe verifiable agent identities will become infrastructure, not an experiment.

5. Simulate one contract cycle with an AI agent this week.

If your current tools can't issue verifiable credentials, what happens when a counterparty demands them?
video_script: Contracts signed by AIs gain weight.
Your next hire might be an AI.

script: Contracts signed by AIs gain weight.
Your next hire might be an AI.

instagram.caption:
Your next AI coworker can now legally sign a contract in its own name. 🤖

NewCore just raised $66M to issue verifiable IDs for autonomous agents—turning them into legal actors. 📄 Contracts signed by AIs can now hold legal weight, without a human behind them.

This changes hiring: companies are already testing agents that negotiate deals as themselves.

If your next hire has no human name, who ensures their credentials are backed by your business? 🔐

Investors believe verifiable agent identities become infrastructure, not just an experiment.
